info.text: In this demo we'll create a function that uses the Web's built-in Web Audio API to load and play an audio file. We could do this with a simple audio element, but the Web Audio API will give us lower-level control than we would get from that element.
info.title: getting started
nn library
info.text: I'm importing the netnet-standard-library (nn.min.js) to make it easier to create and center a button element. Of course, you can do all of this with the browser's native APIs, JavaScript libraries are just abstractions that reduce boilerplate and let us express our ideas with less code.

info.title: AudioContext
info.text: We start by creating an AudioContext, which is the browser's audio engine. I've defined it globally in case I want to use it in other functions later on.

info.title: playSound
info.text: Then I'll create a function which will both load and play the sound. The function is marked async so we can await file loading before decoding and playing.

info.title: Web Audio Nodes
info.text: I'll use our AudioContext, which I named ctx, to create a BufferSource, which I named src, that will hold decoded audio. I've also used the AudioContext to create a GainNode to control volume.

info.title: audio graph
info.text: We connect the nodes to build an "audio graph", the ctx.destination will be whatever you have your computer's audio settings outputting to, likely your speakers or headphones, so the graph looks like this: src → gn → output.

info.title: fetch file
info.text: We download the mp3 file, created by orangefreesounds, using the browser's fetch() API and wait for it to finish. Then res.arrayBuffer() turns the file into raw bytes of data the decoder can understand.

info.title: decode and play
info.text: The Web Audio API's decodeAudioData() method converts those bytes into an AudioBuffer. Inside the callback we assign it to our src node's buffer property and call src.start() to begin playback.

info.title: user action
info.text: Most browsers require a user action to start audio, which is why I'm calling playSound() from a click event.

info.title: other ways
info.text: As I mentioned earlier, we could load and play sound with the audio element too, but the Web Audio API gives us low-level building blocks like gain, filters, panning, effects, precise scheduling, looping, playback-rate control, and analyzers, that we can wire into a custom graph like this. If you want higher-level tools, we could use Tone.js which wraps Web Audio with ready-made synthesizers and samplers, a transport/tempo timeline, sequencing utilities, envelopes/LFOs, and effect chains.
